edges.description: TALDO1 variants reduce or abolish measurable transaldolase enzyme activity.
Loss of transaldolase activity disrupts nonoxidative pentose-phosphate metabolism.
Pentose-phosphate-pathway imbalance disrupts NADPH and reactive oxygen intermediate handling.
TALDO deficiency causes diagnostic urinary polyol and seven-carbon sugar abnormalities.
edges.target: Increased urinary polyols and seven-carbon sugars

edges.description: The transaldolase metabolic defect is associated with abnormal circulating glutamine concentration.
Pentose-phosphate metabolite accumulation is associated with renal involvement in transaldolase deficiency.
intermediate_mechanisms: Toxic effects of accumulated sugars, sugar phosphates, and polyols on proximal tubules.

edges.description: Severe antenatal transaldolase deficiency can present with hydrops fetalis.
Severe antenatal or neonatal transaldolase deficiency can present with edema.
The multisystem developmental presentation of transaldolase deficiency can include dysmorphic facial features.
Transaldolase deficiency can include neurodevelopmental delay through incompletely defined intermediates.
Transaldolase deficiency is associated with genital developmental abnormalities through unknown intermediates.
The multisystem developmental presentation of transaldolase deficiency can include congenital heart disease.
The multisystem developmental presentation of transaldolase deficiency can include congenital aortic disease.
Transaldolase deficiency can include cardiac hypertrophy through incompletely defined intermediates.
Severe multisystem transaldolase deficiency can include respiratory physiology abnormalities.
The multisystem developmental presentation of transaldolase deficiency can include cutis laxa and premature skin wrinkling through incompletely defined intermediates.
Transaldolase deficiency can include hypergonadotropic hypogonadism through incompletely defined endocrine intermediates.
Oxidative and mitochondrial stress increases hepatocyte cell death.
Recurrent hepatocyte injury and cell death promotes progressive hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis.
Hepatocyte injury and fibrosis produce cirrhosis.
Severe liver-centered transaldolase deficiency is associated with hepatosplenomegaly, but the cached evidence does not specify the intermediate mechanism.
Severe liver-centered transaldolase deficiency is associated with thrombocytopenia, but the cached evidence does not specify the intermediate mechanism.
Severe liver-centered transaldolase deficiency is associated with anemia, but the cached evidence does not specify the intermediate mechanism.
Liver damage is associated with spider angiomas and visible skin vessels.
intermediate_mechanisms: Liver damage with visible skin-vessel changes.

edges.description: Hepatic dysfunction in transaldolase deficiency is associated with increased serum bile acids.
Reduced transaldolase enzyme activity directly reports the primary TALDO1 catalytic-function defect.
Increased urinary polyols and seven-carbon sugars report the downstream pentose-phosphate metabolite imbalance caused by transaldolase deficiency.
edges.target: Increased urinary polyols and seven-carbon sugars

nodes.description: Ongoing liver injury in transaldolase deficiency produces progressive nodular hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis. This chronic liver remodeling explains major liver-centered consequences, including hepatosplenomegaly, cytopenias, bleeding tendency, and skin vascular changes associated with liver damage.

evidence.reference_title: The pathogenesis of transaldolase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: liver cirrhosis which results from increased cell death of hepatocytes

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and molecular characteristics of two transaldolase-deficient patients.
evidence.snippet: rapidly progressive nodular liver fibrosis, tubulopathy and severe clotting disturbances

nodes.description: Transaldolase deficiency increases hepatocyte cell death. The cited pathogenesis review links complete human TAL deficiency to cirrhosis through increased hepatocyte cell death, placing hepatocyte apoptosis between redox/mitochondrial stress and chronic liver remodeling.

evidence.reference_title: The pathogenesis of transaldolase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: liver cirrhosis which results from increased cell death of hepatocytes

nodes.description: Early liver transplantation has been proposed for severe progressive liver disease, but the available cached evidence is cautious and not sufficient to establish benefit.

evidence.snippet: Liver transplantation in the early stage of the disease could perhaps be useful.

nodes.description: N-acetylcysteine has model-organism support for preventing acetaminophen- induced liver failure and limited human observational support for lowering alpha-fetoprotein, so it is recorded as a context-specific antioxidant option rather than established disease-modifying therapy.

nodes.description: The pentose phosphate pathway supplies NADPH for antioxidant defense. Transaldolase-dependent balancing of NADPH and reactive oxygen intermediates influences mitochondrial membrane potential, ATP synthesis, and cell survival; impaired redox handling is implicated in liver injury and acetaminophen sensitivity.

evidence.reference_title: The pathogenesis of transaldolase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Balancing of NADPH and ROI levels by the PPP enzyme transaldolase

evidence.reference_title: Apparent Acetaminophen Toxicity in a Patient with Transaldolase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: these hepatic complications are accentuated by oxidative stress related to acetaminophen administration

evidence.reference_title: Deletion of Ser-171 causes inactivation, proteasome-mediated degradation and complete deficiency of human transaldolase.
evidence.snippet: no expression of the TALDeltaS171 protein or enzyme activity was detected

readouts.interpretation: Reduced transaldolase enzyme activity directly reports the primary TALDO1 catalytic-function defect.

nodes.description: Disease-specific management is supportive and should include avoidance of acetaminophen because clinical and model evidence indicates increased vulnerability to acetaminophen-related oxidative liver injury.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and molecular characteristics of two transaldolase-deficient patients.
evidence.snippet: Symptomatic treatment can be proposed.

evidence.reference_title: Apparent Acetaminophen Toxicity in a Patient with Transaldolase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: transaldolase-deficient patients are uniquely sensitive to acetaminophen and should avoid this antipyretic.

nodes.description: Biallelic TALDO1 variants reduce or abolish human transaldolase protein stability and catalytic activity. The Ser171 deletion mechanism shows that mutant transaldolase can be translated but rapidly degraded by the proteasome and lacks appreciable enzymatic activity.
